figures.caption: Figure 9 Box plot of per-user IGF1 scores by model on the survey dataset. The red dashed line shows the overall median. Even top models have users at IGF1 = 0, showing substantial within-model variance.

Table 12 Evidence objects cited by the model for two predicted interests. Each object includes the content description (hashtags and title) and the user’s engagement type, classified as implicit positive (imp+: watch ≥30s), explicit positive (exp+: like, share), or implicit negative (imp−: skip).

Figure 2 Comparison of synthetic and real UIH distributions. (a) Per-user engagement count densities show similar right-skewed patterns. (b) Box plots confirm that the relative ordering across action types is preserved.

Figure 1 Overview of the GISTBench evaluation pipeline. User interaction histories are processed by an LLM to produce predicted interests. These are evaluated on two axes: Interest Groundedness (IG, decomposed into precision and recall) and Interest Specificity (IS, computed over verified categories only), then mapped to standardized taxonomy categories for normalization.

Figure 3 IG Precision vs. IG Recall per model on the survey dataset. Bubble size encodes the average number of predicted interest categories; color encodes IGF1. Dotted curves are iso-F1 contours at 0.1–0.5. All models cluster in the low-recall region (IGR < 0.4), confirming that coverage is the universal bottleneck. DeepSeek-R1 achieves the highest precision (57.3%) and IGF1 (42.9%). Qwen2.5-72B offers the best balance between precision and recall among capable models.

Table 5 Survey-Based metrics (S-Prec, S-Rec) versus proposed metrics (IGP , IGR, IGF1, IS), all in %. S-Prec and S-Rec are computed against user self-reported interests; IG and IS are computed from engagement signals (Section 4). The full precision-recall decomposition reveals that IGP ≫ IGR on the survey dataset, consistent with the benchmark results (Table 4): coverage, not hallucination, is the bottleneck. ρ: Spearman correlation between survey F1 and geometric mean of IGF1 and IS. †Llama-3.1-8B metrics are based on only 20 users; the majority of its generations were unparsable even after best-effort regex extraction and JSON repair, yielding too few valid outputs for the remaining survey users.

Figure 4 Per-user IG Precision vs. IG Recall on the benchmark datasets, colored by model. Diamond markers show per-model medians; small dots show individual users (α = 0.15). The diagonal reference line (y = x) highlights the universal asymmetry IGP > IGR. Within-model variance is substantial: even top models have many users with IGP < 0.5. This reflects user-level difficulty variation.

Figure 8 Taxonomy coverage across datasets for (a) GPT-OSS-120B alone and (b) the union of all evaluated models. The red dashed line marks the total 325 taxonomy categories. Ensembling substantially improves coverage on mid-range datasets (e.g., MIND: 53% → 75%; Goodreads: 59% → 76%) but has limited effect where a single model already saturates (Synthetic) or where data sparsity fundamentally constrains discovery (Amazon Music).

Figure 7 Cumulative distribution of predicted interests across taxonomy categories (sorted by size, largest first). The steep initial rise confirms heavy concentration: 10.1% of categories capture 50% of all predicted interests, and 29.4% capture 80%, reflecting the long-tailed nature of user engagement.

Table 2 Examples of top-level interest domains and their corresponding interest categories. Each domain contains several fine-grained interest categories; only a representative subset is shown. The full list of categories is provided in Appendix A.
Table 9 Interest categories used for taxonomy normalization (L–Y), continued.
